rulings.Case Title: CAVALRY PORTFOLIO SERVICES, LLC, AS ASSIGNEE OF VS. DENNIS M BULLOCK et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SET ASIDE/VACATE JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 26, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT DENNIS BULLOCK's MOTION FOR SET ASIDE/VACATE JUDGMENT Defendant Bullock's motion to set aside and vacate judgment is GRANTED. As Bullock seeks to vacate renewal of the judgment under CCP §683.170, he is not seeking equitable relief in a collateral proceeding and the limitations periods for CCP §§473 and 473.5 do not apply. Failure to serve process is a ground to vacate renewal of a judgment, and Bullock filed this motion within the 30-day statutory period. (See Fidelity Creditor Service, Inc. v. Browne (2001) 89 Cal.App.4th 195, 200-02.) Bullock says he did not get actual notice until he received the notice of renewed judgment, but even assuming Bullock had notice when his wages were first garnished, "actual knowledge of the judgment [does] not govern the timeliness of his section 683.170 motion." (Id. at 204.) A §683.170 motion requires no showing of diligence or a meritorious defense in the underlying action. (Id. at 203-06.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Bullock is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ru

rulings.Case Title: CECILIA CONTRERAS ET AL VS. CALVIN COOLEDGE WILEY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 26, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT CALVIN WILEY's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant Wiley's demurrer to the second cause of action for "GENERAL NEGLIGENCE" is SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. Plaintiff can recover her emotional distress or general damages flowing from the car accident that caused physical injury on her first cause of action for motor vehicle negligence. The second cause of action appears to seek emotional distress damages based on Wiley's negligent use of a police code that caused numerous officers to respond to the scene. (Amended Complaint, par. 19.) Wiley's post-accident conduct did not cause further physical injury and the law limits the recovery of emotional distress damages in the absence of physical injury. (See Burgess v. Sup. Crt. (1992) 2 Cal.4th 1064, 1076-1077.) The circumstances of this case do not allow the recovery of emotional distress damages based on the negligent use of a police code. The allegations of the amended complaint also fail to show that plaintiff can amend to plead a cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Wiley is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ru

rulings.Case Title: OMAR SHAFIE VS. MEGHAN MCGARRY DANGANAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Jobe Danganan, Meghan Mcgarry Notice Of Defendants' Motion To Quash Plaintiff'S Deposition Subpoena To Ucsf Health Information Management And To Grant A Protective Order And Sanctions (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, October 26, 2018, Line 1 Defendants Jobe Danganan, Meghan Mcgarry'S Motion To Quash Plaintiff'S Deposition Subpoena To Ucsf Health Information Management And To Grant A Protective Order And Sanctions (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2) The subpoena is directed to a side issue of the side issue: speculation about whether defendant McGarry, who works as a research scientist at UCSF, might have accessed plaintiff's medical records to come to the conclusion that the claimed disability involved defendant's knee and that defendant was not "really disabled." Under the circumstances presented, it appears unreasonable to issue this third party subpoena to UCSF, technically due after the close of fact discovery, that does not relate to the claims and defenses in this action, and that is not reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The undersigned agrees with plaintiff that defendants' additional request for a protective order "to relieve defendant McGarry any further discovery obligations" to curtail defendant's obligation to respond to discovery appears to be an attempt to revisit the Court's prior ruling to allow additional discovery on this defendant and is not supported by a separate statement as required under CRC Rule 3.1345. For these reasons, this portion of the motion should be denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tim@ncalegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: OMAR SHAFIE VS. MEGHAN MCGARRY DANGANAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Defendants' Motion To Quash Plaintiff'S Deposition Subpoena To Ucsf Health Information Management And To Grant A Protective Order And Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, October 26, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT JOBE DANGANAN, MEGHAN MCGARRY'S' Motion To Quash Plaintiff'S Deposition Subpoena To Ucsf Health Information Management And To Grant A Protective Order And Sanctions Pro Tem Judge J. Timothy Nardell, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion should be granted in part and denied in part. The undersigned agrees with defendants that the subpoena directed to UCSF is not reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence, and is subject to limitation under the Discovery Act. (CCP §§ 2017.010, 2017.020.) The Complaint alleges that defendants improperly terminated plaintiff's tenancy at the downstairs unit of the property that defendants purchased using the "owner move in" provisions of the San Francisco Rent Ordinance, on the representation that defendant McGarry intended to occupy that downstairs unit as her primary residence, when in fact both defendants have occupied the upstairs unit as their primary residence. The subpoena appears to have been engendered by discovery into a side issue which was not alleged in the complaint, and has dubious relevance. During discovery, both sides have appeared interested in testing plaintiff's assertion that he had a disability as a basis for claiming additional move-out compensation. This issue is not relevant to any claims or defenses in this case, as far as the undersigned can tell. If plaintiff's disability were challenged, it would at best, be a credibility issue, and might not even be admissible. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)
rulings.Judge: Judge Pro Tem: J. Timothy Nardell

rulings.Case Title: JAMES GAYAPA VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce The Court'S Orders And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, October 26, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion To Enforce The Court'S Orders And Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge J. Timothy Nardell, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion should be granted. The motion to enforce the Court's prior orders and for sanctions is unopposed. The Court's prior order to compel responses to defendant's written discovery stated that plaintiff would face a terminating sanction if he failed to comply with the Court's order. (Order Granting Defendant's Motion To Enforce the Court's Order and Request for Discovery, dated August 21, 2018, Lake Decl. Exh. I.) The record indicates that plaintiff was present at the hearing that led to this Order, and that this prior Order was served on plaintiff. Under the circumstances, the Court believes that a terminating sanction is appropriate. (Lake Decl. at 12, 13.) Defendant is requested to provide a form of order that recites the procedural history that led to the request for terminating sanctions, and the Court's tentative ruling, as set forth above. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tim@ncalegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: BRETT PENFIL VS. SUSAN BADGER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Susan Badger'S Notice Motion And Motion To Modify Subpoena For Production Of Business Records
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, October 26, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANT SUSAN BADGER'S Motion To Modify Subpoena For Production Of Business Records Pro Tem Judge J. Timothy Nardell, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion should be denied. The subpoena at issue appears to the Court to be within the scope of permissible discovery - relevant to the subject matter of the case and reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The undersigned agrees with defendant that the unrestricted timeframe of the subpoena could make it overly broad or burdensome. However, it is not clear that a subpoena simply seeking an architect's records for address of the property at issue in the case without a date restriction would necessarily be overly broad. To the extent that the subpoena could be deemed to be overly broad or burdensome, these issues should be raised by the third party witness with facts to evidence why the requests in the subpoena are unduly burdensome. The undersigned does agree that private personal information of defendants and third parties, such as social security numbers and financial information, is not relevant to the issues presented in this case. Such information may be redacted by the third party witness prior to production, and should be redacted by the parties prior to the admission of any of the subpoenaed documents into the public record. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tim@ncalegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DESOTO CAB COMPANY, INC. VS. GERALD MICHAEL PACHECO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 26, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANTS MIKE PACHECO and GERALD PACHECO's MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND Defendant Gerald Michael Pacheco's motion to vacate default and default judgment is DENIED. Pacheco fails to demonstrate good cause for relief under CCP § 473.5. At paragraph 12 of this declaration, Pacheco admits that he knew of the lawsuit approximately three months prior to the entry of the default. Pacheco fails to demonstrate good cause for relief under CCP § 473. He does not explain how mistake, surprise, inadvertence, or excusable neglect caused the default to be entered. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ru
